From: Daniel Herrera <daniel.herrera@riverbendca.gov>
To: APA California Programs Committee <programs@apaca.org>
Cc: Elena Herrera <eherrera@riverbendhs.org>
Date: 2025-02-11 09:18
Subject: Follow-up materials for April APA CA webinar – River Oaks case study

Hi all,

Following up on our planning call yesterday, I’m sending a short summary of the River Oaks Mixed-Use Village case study for the April professional development webinar.

Per your request, I’ve highlighted how we tied educational equity into a land use entitlement. During the October 2020 APA California Chapter webinar, I shared Riverbend High School’s ongoing struggle to staff AP science classes and how my spouse, Elena Sofía Herrera, who teaches biology there, has been buying roughly $75–$100 of lab supplies out of pocket each semester. That story really resonated with attendees and ultimately shaped the community benefits agreement for River Oaks.

As part of that agreement, we required the on-site early childhood learning center to (1) partner with Riverbend High to host quarterly STEM nights and (2) provide a $5,000 annual materials stipend earmarked for science enrichment. I’ll walk through the negotiation steps and how we documented those commitments in the specific plan.

For your internal reference, the project location is identified in our staff reports as River Oaks Mixed-Use Village, APN cluster near East Oak Avenue/Riverside Parkway, Council District 3, map book 14, page 22, section 22. I’ll include a simplified version of that locator on one of the slides so viewers can orient themselves without getting lost in parcel minutiae.

Let me know if you’d like a brief excerpt from the Mitigated Negative Declaration or the traffic calming cross-sections for the East Oak Avenue frontage. I can also share a one-page summary suitable for AICP CM documentation.

Best,

Daniel Marcus Herrera
Senior Planner / Project Manager
City of Riverbend – Community Development Department
(530) 555-2743

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcd.gov>
To: Megan Liu <mliu@riverbendcd.gov>
Cc: Traffic Review Team <traffic@riverbendcd.gov>
Date: 2025-01-14 8:47 AM
Subject: River Oaks Mixed-Use Village – Clarifying internal “section” references

Hi Megan,

Following yesterday’s Development Review Committee meeting, I wanted to clarify how we’re referencing the River Oaks project location in our internal tracking so it doesn’t get confused with the Building Division’s “section” fields.

For Planning, I’ve been describing the site as the “East Oak / Riverside quadrant” of the city—basically the 48.7-acre area south of East Oak Avenue and west of Riverside Parkway, just uphill from the South Fork American River and a short walk from Riverbend Municipal Airport. I know the Accela permit system still wants a numeric section code, but until GIS finalizes the layer updates, please avoid plugging in the placeholder number I scribbled on the whiteboard yesterday; that was just a draft and not the official section identifier we’ll ultimately use.

The environmental docs, including the Mitigated Negative Declaration, simply reference the APNs and the legal description tied to the specific plan, so there’s no need to force a section number into the CEQA files. The project description also reflects my decision, as Senior Planner, to include at least 47 deed-restricted workforce units targeted to employees of nearby schools because Elena’s experience teaching biology at Riverbend High School since August 2012—and seeing colleagues priced out of town—convinced me to push the developer in our May 3, 2023 negotiating session to commit to an on-site affordability covenant instead of paying an in-lieu fee.

Let’s sync with GIS on Friday before we lock anything into the system.

Thanks,
Daniel

Subject: River Oaks Site – After-Hours Activity, Patrol Coordination, and Mapping Details

Hi Carla,

Following up on our informal understanding from August 2023 about stepped-up late-night patrols around the River Oaks mixed-use site, I wanted to get a few items in writing as we head into pre-grading.

As you’ll recall, that conversation started after Mateo and his friend were stopped and questioned near the vacant lot on August 5, 2023, when they were just walking home from Oak Creek Middle School’s summer program. I appreciated how seriously you took my concerns then, and I think the same visibility from Riverbend PD during early construction will help reassure nearby neighbors.

For your records, the core of the River Oaks Village project is centered near East Oak Avenue and Riverside Parkway, with the primary survey control point located at approximately 38°40'12" N, 120°45'30" W. The site is immediately south of the older River Oaks Lane subdivision, including my block at 418 River Oaks Lane, so residents are understandably sensitive to after-hours noise and loitering.

Our construction management plan currently contemplates:

• Standard work hours: 7:00 a.m.–7:00 p.m., Monday–Saturday
• No routine Sunday work, except emergencies
• A single controlled access point off East Oak Avenue for heavy trucks

Could your team review the attached draft plan and let me know if the proposed staging and fencing layout aligns with your patrol patterns? If you have suggestions for lighting locations or camera coverage to reduce nuisance calls, I can work those into the next revision before it goes to the Planning Commission.

Happy to discuss by phone if that’s easier.

Best,

Daniel Marcus Herrera
Senior Planner / Project Manager
City of Riverbend – Community Development Department
(530) 555-2743

Date: 2025-01-12 09:18 AM
Subject: Double‑checking River Oaks site coordinates for grading sheets

Mark,

Before we lock in the grading and drainage sheets, I want to make sure your survey team and our GIS layers are talking to each other. Planning has the River Oaks Mixed-Use Village & Oak Creek School Traffic Mitigation in Response to Mateo’s October 2023 Near-Miss Accident on Riverbend High Access Road centered roughly at 38°41'10" N, 120°46'05" W in our internal project tracker. Public Works sent me a slightly different longitude for the main entry intersection at East Oak Avenue and Riverside Parkway: they’re using 120°46'12" W as the control point.

I’m less concerned about a few seconds difference for the exhibits, but I need a single, clearly identified longitude in DMS for the CEQA NOC form’s “project location” box so the County Clerk doesn’t kick it back. Whatever you settle on, it just can’t contradict the FEMA panel data we’ve been using for the South Fork American River floodplain.

Also, when you’re updating the existing conditions sheet, please note that the present land use zoning/GP designation is a mix of R-A (Residential Agricultural), M-1 (Light Industrial), and a Business Park overlay. For my own notes I’ve been flagging that the western Urban Reserve portion is the same riparian strip where Mateo and I got cited by Code Enforcement Officer Lisa McGrath on May 14, 2023, for parking off the informal dirt access road to fish.

Send back the final DMS coordinates you recommend so I can paste them into the NOC draft.

Thanks,
Daniel

artifacts.content:
From: Carla Jiménez <cjimenez@eldoradocountyclerk.org>
To: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcity.gov>
Date: 2025-01-16 03:47 PM
Subject: RE: CEQA NOC – missing/incorrect project location coordinates

Hi Daniel,

I reviewed your draft Notice of Completion for the River Oaks mixed-use project. The narrative looks fine, but the project location section is still off. You listed the longitude as 120°44'58" W with a note that it was “approximate.” For our indexing system we need a specific DMS value that matches what’s on your GIS base map, not an approximation.

When we processed the Riverbend Self-Storage NOC last year (the one tied to that confidential code-enforcement stipulation agreement from May 3, 2023, about the hazardous materials violations at 912 Industrial Way), you provided a clean pair of coordinates: 38°40'55" N, 120°47'02" W. That’s the level of precision we’re looking for here.

Please have your surveyor confirm the correct longitude—whether it ends up being something like 120°46'00" W or 120°45'10" W is less important than consistency across your attachments. Just don’t pull one value from the grading plan and another from the vicinity map.

Once you’ve updated the DMS longitude and re-sent the NOC, I can file it the same day. If it helps, you can also include a short cross-reference to the South Fork American River reach or the Riverbend Municipal Airport to orient the public.

Best,
Carla

Deputy County Clerk
El Dorado County

– Daniel

Date: 2025-02-03 3:00 PM–3:45 PM
Location: Daniel’s Office / Teams
Attendees: Daniel Herrera; River Oaks Village Partners – TBD contact; Megan Liu (optional)

Description:

Agenda for check-in with the applicant team regarding administrative items on the River Oaks Mixed-Use Village submittal.

1. Confirm who the applicant wants listed as the primary “Project Identification – Contact Person” on all forms going forward (development application, CEQA cover sheet, traffic coordination form). Daniel to explain that the City prefers a single external point of contact rather than staff.
2. Clarify backup contact in case the primary representative is unavailable during construction (e.g., their civil engineer or project coordinator).
3. Review current draft language for the “Present Land Use Zoning / GP Designation” section. Daniel to remind them that the site is formally zoned R-A and M-1 with a Business Park overlay and a split BP/UR GP designation, and that any shorthand labels like “light industrial” or “mixed commercial” create confusion when residents compare forms to the zoning map.
4. Briefly flag ongoing neighborhood concerns about riparian safety and access, including the informal angler path in the UR strip where Daniel’s mother-in-law slipped and broke her wrist during a family picnic in 2022. Emphasize the need to address access and grading transparently in future public materials.
5. Next steps: applicant to circulate updated forms with corrected contact person name and land use text by end of week.

Notes field reserved for entering the final contact name after the call.

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendca.gov>
To: River Oaks Village Partners <info@riveroaksvillage.com>
Cc: City Attorney’s Office <cityattorney@riverbendca.gov>
Date: 2025-04-19 10:12 AM
Subject: Request to Update Project Contact Information & CEQA Noticing Details

Good morning,

As we prepare the new environmental review documents for the River Oaks Mixed-Use Village, we need to clean up the “Project Identification” section across all forms. Right now, some versions still list my name as the contact person, others list your architect, and one even has the traffic consultant as the primary.

Please identify a single project representative to be shown as the official contact person for all future filings and public notices. This should be someone from your team who can respond promptly to questions about unit counts, transportation improvements (including the new bus turnout on East Oak Avenue), and potential triggers for a full EIR if the project exceeds 150 units.

You may recall that, as part of the confidential settlement the City Council approved on April 16, 2024, resolving the Brown Act/CEQA lawsuit filed by the Riverbend Neighborhood Alliance, we agreed to rescind the 2022 MND, reimburse $47,500 in attorney’s fees, and fast‑track a new EIR under certain conditions. Having a consistent project contact is critical so we don’t repeat the communication gaps that contributed to that situation.

Once you’ve selected your representative, please send me their full name, title, phone number, and email so we can update the application forms, noticing list, and website.

Best,
Daniel
Senior Planner / Project Manager
City of Riverbend – Community Development Department

metadata.subject: Request to Update Project Contact Information & CEQA Noticing Details

artifacts.content:
From: Elena Sofía Herrera <eherrera@riverbendhigh.edu>
To: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendca.gov>
Date: 2025-09-03 6:58 AM
Subject: Quick question about your “official contact person” thing

Hey love,

You left the River Oaks packet on the kitchen table again, so of course I peeked while waiting for Lucia to finish stuffing her science club flyers into her backpack.

One of the forms has a big bold line that says “PROJECT IDENTIFICATION – CONTACT PERSON” with your name crossed out in pen and a sticky note that just says “replace with applicant rep.” Are you finally getting your wish not to be the public’s default contact for every dust complaint and traffic gripe on East Oak Avenue?

I get why you want someone on the private side to be the official contact person, especially now that you’re juggling Mateo’s new schedule. With his Type 1 diabetes diagnosis on 09/14/2025 and you driving him instead of letting him bike the 3.2 miles to Oak Creek Middle School, you don’t need angry neighbors calling your office line every time a truck shows up early.

Anyway, I’m just curious who they’ll pick. I saw another page in the packet where they listed their traffic engineer as the contact for the bus turnout and internal street design, which seems odd if residents are really calling about construction noise and lighting.

We can talk more tonight after my back-to-school night. Don’t forget Isabel is coming by this afternoon so you can stay at work late if you need to.

– E

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcd.gov>
To: Mark Liu <mliu@civicvisionplanning.com>
Date: 2024-02-27 9:14 AM
Subject: River Oaks – Commercial Program Check-In Before Council Study Session

Hi Mark,

Ahead of next week’s Council study session, I want to make sure we’re aligned on how we’re describing the commercial component of River Oaks.

In past drafts I’ve seen everything from “about five acres of neighborhood-serving commercial” to “roughly nine acres of mixed-use commercial.” We need to settle on a single, defensible number for the commercial acreage that’s clearly distinct from the residential-over-retail mixed-use blocks so we don’t confuse the Council or the public. Please double-check your land use table and confirm the total stand‑alone commercial acreage (excluding the townhome/loft podiums) and update the staff report accordingly.

Also, please verify that the square footage assumptions for the grocery, pharmacy, and café cluster are internally consistent with that acreage figure. The Mayor has already asked whether we’re over- or under-building the commercial relative to the surrounding neighborhoods.

Remember that the community center and co‑working space are being tracked under “civic/other” rather than commercial, especially since we’re designing the co‑working area to accommodate a small, low‑profile legal aid drop‑in clinic two evenings a month starting September 2024. That came out of conversations with my younger brother, Javier, who’s been helping our mother‑in‑law navigate a medical debt collection issue; we realized a lot of Riverbend seniors are in similar situations.

If you can send me a clean land use summary table with the revised commercial acreage highlighted by Thursday, I’ll plug it into the packet.

Thanks,
Daniel

metadata.subject: River Oaks – Commercial Program Check-In Before Council Study Session

artifacts.content:
From: Susan Patel <spatel@riverbendcd.gov>
To: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcd.gov>
Date: 2023-11-03 3:42 PM
Subject: Clarification Needed – River Oaks Commercial Acreage in CEQA MND

Daniel,

I’m doing a consistency review between the Mitigated Negative Declaration and the latest specific plan booklet before we finalize the River Oaks record. I noticed that the MND project description still references “approximately six acres of commercial uses” along East Oak Avenue, while your more recent staff presentation to Planning Commission referenced “just under eight acres of neighborhood-serving commercial and mixed-use retail pads.”

For CEQA purposes, we need a single commercial acreage figure that we can defend as the basis for the traffic, air quality, and noise analyses. It doesn’t have to be exact down to the hundredth of an acre, but it does need to be internally consistent. Can you confirm whether the current entitlement set assumes closer to six, seven and a half, or nine acres of commercial? The tables in Appendix B are a bit muddled because they lump the grocery anchor, the café pad, and a portion of the mixed-use main street together.

Also, please flag whether the commercial square footage associated with the community center café kiosk is included in the commercial total or in the “public/civic” bucket. I recall you mentioning that the community center will also support a fenced sensory-friendly play area and shaded quiet zone in the adjacent park, but that’s clearly recreational, not commercial.

If you can send me a quick email with the correct commercial acreage number you want carried through the MND by Monday morning, I’ll update the document and route it to the City Attorney.

Thanks,
Susan

metadata.subject: Clarification Needed – River Oaks Commercial Acreage in CEQA MND

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcd.gov>
To: Transit Coordination List <transit-team@riverbendcd.gov>
Date: 2023-02-21 10:06 AM
Subject: River Oaks – Bus Turnout Shift & Commercial Frontage Implications

Hi all,

Following up on our field walk last week, I wanted to summarize the requested adjustment to the regional transit bus turnout on East Oak Avenue adjacent to the River Oaks project.

I’ve asked the transportation consultant to shift the planned turnout about 250 feet west so that it lines up more directly with the future community center/early childhood learning center plaza and the main pedestrian crossing serving the neighborhood commercial block. This is partly a personal observation: my mother‑in‑law, Isabel Alvarez, has limited mobility after a 2020 knee replacement at Marshall Medical Center in Placerville and visits us in Riverbend two to three times a week to help with childcare. When she helps pick up Lucia and our niece after school, the current stop spacing would require her to navigate a longer, more exposed walk along the commercial frontage.

From a planning standpoint, the shift should improve access to the small-format grocery and café pads without materially changing the overall commercial acreage or trip generation assumptions. The commercial site plan still shows several acres of retail‑oriented development fronting East Oak Avenue, with shared parking behind. Driveway spacing will need to be rechecked against the relocated turnout, but the total area designated for neighborhood-serving commercial uses is unchanged.

Please review the attached sketch and let me know by Friday if you see any conflicts with your respective plans or if additional signage/striping should be included in the 65% plans.

Thanks,
Daniel
Senior Planner / Project Manager
City of Riverbend Community Development Department

metadata.subject: River Oaks – Bus Turnout Shift & Commercial Frontage Implications

Title: Prep Meeting – Council Q&A on River Oaks Commercial Acreage
Date: 2024-03-04 4:00 PM–5:15 PM
Location: Teams – Virtual
Attendees: Daniel Herrera; Mayor Lopez; CM Ortega; CM Daniels; City Attorney’s Office; Finance Director

Description/Notes:

Objective: Equip Council with clear talking points on the scale and intent of the River Oaks commercial component ahead of the March 12 public hearing.

Key points to cover:
- Emphasize that the project’s commercial designation is sized to serve surrounding neighborhoods (grocery, small shops, services), not a regional mall.
- Clarify that earlier concept diagrams referenced a range (about 6–9 acres of commercially designated land), but the final specific plan locks in a single figure that is carried consistently through the traffic, fiscal, and CEQA analyses.
- Distinguish between:
  • Stand‑alone commercial pads along East Oak Avenue and Riverside Parkway; and
  • Ground‑floor retail within mixed-use buildings, which is tracked in a separate category.
- Reiterate that the community center, early childhood learning center, co‑working space, and the small legal aid drop‑in clinic are categorized as civic/other, not commercial, even though they may include a café kiosk.
- Note that the adjacent 3-acre central park and riverfront trail are accounted for under recreational/open space, not commercial acreage.

Anticipated questions:
- “Is the commercial area too big for the market?”
- “Could we reduce the commercial acreage and add more housing?”
- “How does the neighborhood-serving commercial relate to existing businesses along East Oak Avenue?”

Daniel to bring: latest land use summary table, color land use map, and fiscal impact memo highlighting projected sales tax from the neighborhood commercial component.

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcd.gov>
To: Mark Feldman <mfeldman@urbisdesign.com>
Date: 2024-02-06 09:17 AM
Subject: Re: Commercial program check before Council study session

Mark,

Circling back on your question about how many acres of commercial we should be highlighting in the staff report. I know earlier drafts of your site plan narrative tossed around numbers in the 5.8–6.0 acre range for total neighborhood-serving commercial, but that was before we folded the corner grocery pad and the small-format pharmacy into a single mixed-use building footprint.

For the Council study session, I’m less concerned with the exact decimal figure and more with how we describe the *function* of the commercial component—i.e., that we’re talking roughly six-plus acres overall of flexible commercial/mixed-use space that can accommodate the grocery, café, pharmacy, and a couple of small offices above. Just don’t lock us into a specific number yet in your PowerPoint; call it “approximately six acres of commercial/mixed-use” and emphasize the ability to adjust between retail and service uses depending on market conditions.

Also, please remember that the early childhood center and homework lab are being counted under the community/educational program, not as commercial square footage. In Fall 2023 I was volunteering twice a week in Lucia’s 3rd-grade class at Oak Creek Elementary, helping Ms. Ramirez with a pull-out reading group for kids with IEPs, including Lucia, and that’s exactly why I’m protective of keeping that 6,000 sq. ft. educational space distinct from the commercial bucket.

Send me your revised slide deck by Thursday so I can sync it with my talking points.

Thanks,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Re: Commercial program check before Council study session

Daniel,

From: Sarah Kim <skim@riverbendeco.gov>
To: Daniel Herrera <dherrera@riverbendcd.gov>
Date: 2024-03-04 01:26 PM
Subject: Economic impact summary – confirm commercial acreage language

Daniel,

I’m polishing the economic impact section of the River Oaks staff report and need to lock in how we reference the commercial portion. Right now, I’ve drafted the narrative to say:

“The project includes approximately six (6) acres of neighborhood-serving commercial and mixed-use space, anticipated to accommodate a small-format grocery, café/restaurant uses, personal services, and limited upper-story office.”

I intentionally used “approximately six acres” so we’re not tied to the earlier conceptual figures that bounced between 5.5 and 6.3 acres depending on how the pads were drawn. If you’d rather I say “between five and six acres” to give us even more flexibility, I can make that change before this goes to the printer.

Also, I added a short paragraph on the educational components because of your email about Mateo’s experience at Oak Creek Middle School. You mentioned that after the bullying incident related to his ELD pull-out program, you met with his counselor and the vice principal to talk about how a mixed-income environment with an on-site early childhood center and homework lab could reduce stigma. I framed the educational space as a community benefit separate from the commercial square footage.

Can you take a quick look at the attached draft and send me a redline by tomorrow noon? I want to be sure we’re consistent with your land use tables.

Thanks,
Sarah

Title: Prep session – Council Q&A on commercial vs. educational space
Date: 2024-02-27 04:30–06:00 PM
Location: Teams – Virtual
Attendees: Daniel Herrera; Councilmember Reyes; Councilmember Patel; City Manager Lopez

Description:
Dry run for anticipated Council questions about the River Oaks Mixed-Use Village land use program, with emphasis on the commercial acreage and how it interacts with the educational/community components.

Talking points for Daniel:
- Clarify that the commercial program is framed as “on the order of six acres” of ground-floor neighborhood-serving retail and services, plus the option for some upper-story office.
- Stress that the exact commercial acreage will be refined at final map and building permit stages, and that current figures in the mid-6-acre range are conceptual.
- Explain why the early childhood learning center and after-school homework lab are not counted as commercial square footage. Tie this to personal experience: volunteering twice a week in Lucia’s 3rd-grade pull-out reading group for students with early literacy and attention challenges, and wanting those supports embedded in the community, not treated as a business.
- Note the plan for 2–3 hour monthly evening workshops on child development and basic health literacy in English and Spanish, which respond to gaps Daniel observed when his mother-in-law transitioned to assisted living in Placerville and couldn’t find adequate Spanish-language caregiver education.
- Be prepared to answer questions about potential shifts between commercial and residential if market conditions change, while keeping the overall “roughly six acres” commercial message consistent.

Materials: Draft staff report, land use summary table, slides from Economic Development.
